question_text: Three liquids are poured into a measuring cylinder: liquid A (density 0.8 g/cm³), liquid B (density 1.2 g/cm³), and liquid C (density 1.0 g/cm³). Assuming the liquids do not mix, explain which liquid will be at the top, which in the middle, and which at the bottom. Then explain why a object with density 0.9 g/cm³ would float in this mixture.
correct_answer: Liquid A (0.8 g/cm³) will be at the top, liquid C (1.0 g/cm³) in the middle, and liquid B (1.2 g/cm³) at the bottom. Liquids arrange by density with the least dense on top. An object with density 0.9 g/cm³ would float because it is less dense than the overall mixture - it is less dense than liquids B and C, so it would rest on top of liquid C (or sink through A and rest on C depending on arrangement).
explanation: This tests understanding that materials arrange themselves in order of density, least dense on top. The object with density 0.9 g/cm³ is denser than liquid A but less dense than liquids B and C, so it would sink through A but float on the boundary between A and C. This requires students to compare multiple densities and apply the floating/sinking principle to a more complex scenario.
